original_abstract: South Africa possesses a storied tradition of prophetic preaching, epitomized by figures such as Desmond Tutu during the struggle against apartheid. However, the post-1994 democratic era has introduced a new crisis of injustice: systemic exclusion and the persistence of massive poverty affecting half the population. This article examines the contemporary South African landscape, characterized by a widening wealth gap, corruption in social grant administration, and a failing state education system. Drawing on qualitative interviews with unemployed youth in townships, the author highlights the profound sense of betrayal and material deprivation felt by those on the margins. The study argues that these conditions necessitate a renewed commitment to prophetic preaching—a specific homiletic mode that requires the preacher to act as a mouthpiece for the marginalized. The article outlines three essential conditions for this practice: a deep-seated solidarity with the poor derived from rigorous social analysis, a robust hermeneutical approach that interprets biblical texts as transformative "word events," and the active support of both local congregations and the broader ecumenical community. Ultimately, prophetic preaching is presented not as a solitary endeavor, but as a vital ecclesiastical response to socio-economic inequality and the ongoing struggle for human dignity.
humanized_abstract: Although preaching in a prophetic style is a well-known tradition in South Africa—think of Desmond Tutu in the context of the anti-apartheid movement—after 1994 the country has had to face an immense new injustice: the social exclusion and extreme poverty of more than half of the population. With descriptions of the huge inequality in the country, the corruption in the handling of social grants, the falling apart of our education system and many more indicators, the researcher portrays through qualitative interviews with youth living in our township areas the feelings of disillusionment and deprivation of the poor and the unemployed. This crisis can and should be addressed through prophetic preaching as a homiletic genre that is defined by a fundamental alienation in which the prophet acts as the mouth of the voiceless. The conditions for effective prophetic preaching are identified as: a) Social solidarity with the poor and the oppressed, based on sound and on-going social analysis; b) An adequate hermeneutic that is able to lift Scripture from being a relic of the past into becoming a word-event which functions as an event which address human existential conditions; and c) A concrete form of support and responsibility from the local churches in the townships and from the ecumenical world of churches in general. Prophetic preaching therefore must become a structural part of the function of the church in the age in which we live, so as to meet the challenge of inequality and to carry on the struggle for a better human existence.
